module-9-debug-and-polish-project.label: Module 9 Debug and Polish Project
module-9-debug-and-polish-project.summary: Turn a score-report utility into a real multi-file project with a helper module, assert-based checks, file-driven test cases, a debugging pass, and a polished final report.

sketch0.title: Move logic into one helper module first

sketch1.title: Use assert checks to catch bugs early

sketch2.title: Refactor only after the tests protect behavior

project.title: Debug and polish the score report project

score_report_project_step_3_file_tests.title: Step 3: Load data and test normal plus edge cases

score_report_project_step_6_run_final_re.title: Step 6: Run the finished score report

score-report-project-step-1-helper-modul.title: Step 1: Create src/score_report.py
score-report-project-step-1-helper-modul.prompt: This project starts by moving the math into a helper module. In `src/score_report.py`, create `average_score(scores)` so `main.py` can import it and use it for one sample list.
score-report-project-step-1-helper-modul.hint: Return the mean of the list with `sum(scores) / len(scores)`.

help.concept: A helper module holds reusable logic so `main.py` can stay focused on running the program.
help.hint_1: Define the function in `src/score_report.py`, not in `main.py`.
help.hint_2: The function only needs to return the average; `main.py` already handles the import.

score-report-project-step-2-assert-check.title: Step 2: Write assert-based checks
score-report-project-step-2-assert-check.prompt: Now give the project a lightweight safety net. Add `score_line(name, scores)` in `src/score_report.py`, then write real `assert` checks in `tests/check_score_report.py` for both `average_score()` and `score_line()`.
score-report-project-step-2-assert-check.hint: Your tests file should import the helpers and compare real results to exact expected strings or numbers.

help.concept: Simple `assert` checks are enough to protect a small project while you keep building it.
help.hint_1: One assertion should check the number returned by `average_score()`.
help.hint_2: Another assertion should check the exact text from `score_line()`, including the `: 90.0` formatting.

score-report-project-step-2-assert-check.starterCode:
from src.score_report import average_score, score_line

# Add assert checks for average_score() and score_line().
print("tests passed")

score-report-project-step-3-file-tests.title: Step 3: Load data and test normal plus edge cases
score-report-project-step-3-file-tests.prompt: The project is ready for real input data now. In `src/score_report.py`, add `load_scores(path)` so it reads `name|score,score` rows from `data/scores.txt`. Then extend `tests/check_score_report.py` with a file-based assertion for the loader and one edge-case average test.
score-report-project-step-3-file-tests.hint: Each loaded row should become `(name, [scores...])` with integer scores.

help.concept: File-driven helpers need tests that check both the parsed structure and tricky numeric cases.
help.hint_1: Split each line once on `|`, then split the score text on commas.
help.hint_2: A good edge case here is a one-score list, because the average should still be that same score.

score-report-project-step-3-file-tests.starterCode:
from src.score_report import average_score, load_scores, score_line

assert average_score([80, 90, 100]) == 90.0
assert score_line("Ava", [80, 90, 100]) == "Ava: 90.0"

# Add one file-based test for load_scores()
# and one edge-case style test for average_score().
print("tests passed")

score-report-project-step-4-debug-loader.title: Step 4: Debug the broken loader
score-report-project-step-4-debug-loader.prompt: A new bug slipped into `load_scores()`: the scores are being stored as text instead of numbers. Fix the loader so the existing assertions pass again for different fixture files.
score-report-project-step-4-debug-loader.hint: The bug is inside the loop that builds the `scores` list.

help.concept: Debugging gets easier when a focused test file points you straight to the wrong value shape.
help.hint_1: Right now each score is appended as a string like `"88"` instead of the integer `88`.
help.hint_2: Convert each split value before you append it to the `scores` list.

score-report-project-step-4-debug-loader.starterCode:
from src.score_report import average_score, load_scores, score_line

assert average_score([80, 90, 100]) == 90.0
assert score_line("Ava", [80, 90, 100]) == "Ava: 90.0"
assert load_scores("data/scores.txt")[0] == ("Ava", [88, 92, 95])
assert average_score([70]) == 70.0

print("tests passed")

score-report-project-step-5-refactor-rep.title: Step 5: Refactor the report safely
score-report-project-step-5-refactor-rep.prompt: The utility works, so this is the cleanup pass. Add `build_report(rows)` in `src/score_report.py`, update `main.py` to print that helper result, and add one assertion that checks the report text stays stable.
score-report-project-step-5-refactor-rep.hint: Let `build_report(rows)` return the whole string so both `main.py` and your tests can use the same helper.

help.concept: A safe refactor extracts repeated output-building logic into one helper while tests guard the visible behavior.
help.hint_1: Start the report with `Class Score Report`, then add one score line per student.
help.hint_2: In `main.py`, load the rows once and print `build_report(rows)` instead of rebuilding the output inline.

score-report-project-step-5-refactor-rep.starterCode:
from src.score_report import average_score, build_report, load_scores, score_line

assert average_score([80, 90, 100]) == 90.0
assert score_line("Ava", [80, 90, 100]) == "Ava: 90.0"
assert load_scores("data/scores.txt")[0] == ("Ava", [88, 92, 95])

# Add one assertion for build_report().
print("tests passed")

score-report-project-step-6-run-final-re.title: Step 6: Run the finished score report
score-report-project-step-6-run-final-re.prompt: Finish the project with one last polish pass. Update `build_report(rows)` so it also adds a final `Class average: ...` line, keep the assert file in place, and make `main.py` print the finished file-driven report.
score-report-project-step-6-run-final-re.hint: Average the per-student averages, then format that final number to one decimal place.

help.concept: A finished project keeps its helper modules and tests while improving the final user-facing output.
help.hint_1: Accumulate each student's average inside the loop that already adds their score line.
help.hint_2: After the loop, divide by `len(rows)` and append `Class average: {class_average:.1f}` to the report.

score-report-project-step-6-run-final-re.starterCode:
from src.score_report import build_report, load_scores

rows = load_scores("data/scores.txt")
print(build_report(rows))

score-report-try-helper-split.title: Try the helper-module split
score-report-try-helper-split.prompt: Practice the helper-module split with a new file. In `src/stats_helper.py`, define `average_value(values)` so `main.py` can import it and store the average of one sample list.
score-report-try-helper-split.hint: Return the mean of the list from `src/stats_helper.py` and keep the import in `main.py`.

help.concept: A helper module keeps one reusable function in its own file so the main program can stay focused on flow.
help.hint_2: Use `sum(values) / len(values)` for the returned average.

try_module_9_debug_and_polish_project_sk.title: Try it yourself
try_module_9_debug_and_polish_project_sk.prompt: Practice the helper-module split with a new file. In `src/stats_helper.py`, define `average_value(values)` so `main.py` can import it and store the average of one sample list.

debug-polish-overview.title: Move logic into one helper module first
debug-polish-overview.bodyMarkdown:
Debugging is calmer when the project has one place for reusable logic and another place for the program flow.

This module project uses a small layout:

```python
main.py
src/score_report.py
tests/check_score_report.py
data/scores.txt
```

A clean first move is putting the average math in `src/score_report.py`:

```python
def average_score(scores):
    return sum(scores) / len(scores)
```

Then `main.py` can import it:

```python
from src.score_report import average_score
```

That separation matters because later you can test the helper directly instead of searching through one giant file.

debugging-with-small-checks.title: Use assert checks to catch bugs early
debugging-with-small-checks.bodyMarkdown:
For a small Python project, plain `assert` statements are often enough to protect the important behavior.

Example:

```python
assert average_score([80, 90, 100]) == 90.0
assert score_line("Ava", [80, 90, 100]) == "Ava: 90.0"
```

What those checks do:

- the first one protects the numeric calculation
- the second one protects the formatting
- together they make debugging more precise

Once the project reads from `data/scores.txt`, you can also assert that the loader returns the right structure.

polish-for-readability.title: Refactor only after the tests protect behavior
polish-for-readability.bodyMarkdown:
Refactoring works best after a few small checks already describe the behavior you want to keep.

Suppose a report starts with inline printing logic in `main.py`. After the helpers are tested, you can move that formatting into one function like `build_report(rows)`.

That gives you two wins:

- `main.py` becomes shorter and easier to read
- your tests can check the same helper that the program uses

A project feels polished when it has this rhythm:

1. build the helper
2. write the checks
3. fix the bug
4. refactor safely
5. run the final result
